From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Anti-diarrheal](/w/index.php?title=Anti-diarrheal&redirect=no "Anti-diarrheal"))

Medication used to manage and reduce diarrhea

Antidiarrheals are a class of [medication](/wiki/Medication "Medication") used primarily to manage and reduce the frequency of [diarrhea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea"). This class of medication predominantly works by slowing digestion, reducing fluid loss, or improving absorption. There are four main classes: [opiates](/wiki/Opiate "Opiate"), [5-HT3](/wiki/5-HT3_receptor "5-HT3 receptor") receptor antagonists, [adsorbents](/wiki/Adsorption "Adsorption"), and bulk-forming agents.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Commonly used medications include [loperamide](/wiki/Loperamide "Loperamide") (Imodium), [diphenoxylate](/wiki/Diphenoxylate "Diphenoxylate"), [bismuth subsalicylate](/wiki/Bismuth_subsalicylate "Bismuth subsalicylate") (Pepto-Bismol), [Cholestyramine](/wiki/Colestyramine "Colestyramine"), and [Octreotide](/wiki/Octreotide "Octreotide").[[2]](#cite_note-:02-2) Although not considered an antidiarrheal, [oral rehydration solutions](/wiki/Oral_rehydration_therapy "Oral rehydration therapy") are also an important aspect of managing diarrhea.[[2]](#cite_note-:02-2)

## Medical use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Medical use")]

### Acute diarrhea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Acute diarrhea")]

Acute diarrhea is a common condition that typically resolves on its own with [oral rehydration therapy](/wiki/Oral_rehydration_therapy "Oral rehydration therapy").[[2]](#cite_note-:02-2) Most cases of acute diarrhea are caused by infections from contaminated food or water and usually go away on their own within a week.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The most common causes of acute diarrhea in children are the viral agents [norovirus](/wiki/Norovirus "Norovirus") and [rotavirus](/wiki/Rotavirus "Rotavirus"), accounting for about 70% of cases.[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Travelers’ diarrhea](/wiki/Travelers%27_diarrhea "Travelers' diarrhea") (TD) is one of the most common illnesses affecting people of all ages abroad, with up to 70% of travelers developing symptoms within two weeks.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) While traditional advice like avoiding uncooked or unpeeled foods was once thought to be effective, poor sanitation and food handling practices—especially in local eateries—remain major risk factors.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

Anti-motility medications like loperamide and diphenoxylate can help manage the symptoms of travelers’ diarrhea by reducing the frequency of bowel movements, which can be helpful when needing to travel, but are not curative.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) Loperamide and diphenoxylate should be avoided in people with bloody diarrhea or a fever, and loperamide is typically not recommended for children under six.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) Additionally, [zinc supplements](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), particularly in children, can reduce diarrheal duration by up to 25% and reduce stool volume by up to 30%.[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6)

### Dehydration and oral replacement therapy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Dehydration and oral replacement therapy")]

The primary risk from diarrhea is [dehydration](/wiki/Dehydration "Dehydration") and [electrolyte](/wiki/Electrolyte "Electrolyte") loss, making fluid and electrolyte replacement the top treatment priority.[[2]](#cite_note-:02-2) Drinking fluids orally is typically as effective as IV fluids and more cost-efficient for most patients.[[2]](#cite_note-:02-2) Thus, rehydration is essential when managing acute diarrhea, especially in vulnerable groups like young children, older adults, and those with chronic conditions.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) Oral rehydration solutions are made with clean water, salt, and sugar.[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6) These solutions are ideal for severe cases, while milder dehydration can be managed with safe, preferred fluids—though overly sugary drinks should be avoided.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

Dehydration is categorized into three levels: **severe, some, or none**. **Severe dehydration** includes signs like [lethargy](/wiki/Lethargy "Lethargy"), sunken eyes, little to no urine output, and confusion. **Some dehydration** may present with dry mouth, restlessness, thirst, and slightly sunken eyes. If these signs are absent or insufficient, the person is not considered dehydrated.[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6)

### Chronic diarrhea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Chronic diarrhea")]

Chronic diarrhea often persists for greater than a week and may require further work-up from a medical professional.[[2]](#cite_note-:02-2) When the underlying cause cannot be directly addressed, long-term symptom management using antidiarrheals is often necessary.[[2]](#cite_note-:02-2)

## Adverse effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Adverse effects")]

### Opiates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Opiates")]

Loperamide is effective and safe for treating chronic diarrhea. [Diphenoxylate](/wiki/Diphenoxylate "Diphenoxylate") and [difenoxin](/wiki/Difenoxin "Difenoxin") work similarly but can affect the brain at high doses, so they're combined with [atropine](/wiki/Atropine "Atropine") to reduce misuse risks. Stronger opiates like [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") or [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine") can treat severe diarrhea, but they're rarely prescribed due to the risk of misuse, and careful monitoring is needed.[[7]](#cite_note-:12-7) While generally safe, even when combined with antibiotics, the use of opiates may slightly increase the risk of acquiring antibiotic-resistant bacteria.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

### Bismuth subsalicylate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bismuth subsalicylate")]

[Bismuth subsalicylate](/wiki/Bismuth_subsalicylate "Bismuth subsalicylate") is commonly used for diarrhea, but long-term use raises safety concerns and should be monitored.[[7]](#cite_note-:12-7) Bismuth can cause common side effects such as nausea, a bitter taste, diarrhea, and darkened stools.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Since it is a heavy metal, in may cause [encephalopathy](/wiki/Encephalopathy "Encephalopathy") in rare cases.[[2]](#cite_note-:02-2)

### Bile acid resins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Bile acid resins")]

Bile acid binding resins like [cholestyramine](/wiki/Colestyramine "Colestyramine"), [colestipol](/wiki/Colestipol "Colestipol"), and [colesevelam](/wiki/Colesevelam "Colesevelam") are effective but can cause constipation and may interfere with the absorption of other medications, so they should be taken at least two hours apart from other drugs.[[7]](#cite_note-:12-7)

### Alpha-2 (α2) adrenergic agonists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Alpha-2 (α2) adrenergic agonists")]

[Clonidine](/wiki/Clonidine "Clonidine"), used for diabetic diarrhea, is often limited by its ability to lower blood pressure.[[7]](#cite_note-:12-7)

### 5-HT3 antagonists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antidiarrheal&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 5-HT3 antagonists")]

[Alosetron](/wiki/Alosetron "Alosetron"), often used for IBS-related diarrhea, poses a risk of colonic ischemia and severe constipation, which makes it infrequently used.[[7]](#cite_note-:12-7)
